original_reasoning: ملأه and ستره both support taking in or covering over, a decent path to receive.
Arabic trailing or hanging cloth plausibly meets Greek sandal through the notion of something hanging below on the body.

التيه: في مواضع الحيرة / مفازة تيهاء تحيّر سالكوها (in places of bewilderment / a desert where travelers are confused/lost). λήθω=to be hidden, to cause forgetfulness, escape notice. Being lost in a desert = disappearing from knowledge = λήθη (oblivion). The lost-forgotten-hidden cluster is tightly connected.
Arabic gloss identifies a plant; Greek is also a plant. This is direct botanical overlap, though not species-identical.
Arabic صأصل/صوصلاء is a plant name; σέσελις is hartwort plant. Both are plant names with similar phonetics, plausible borrowing
Kafan - spinning wool/burial shroud vs 'fuller's teasel, used for cloth carding' - shared wool/cloth processing
Arabic describes coarse slack physicality; the Greek form plausibly preserves a loosened or relaxed core.

Arabic الإسكاف: shoemaker/craftsman. Greek σκάφη: bowl/tub/basin. Both involve shaped hollowed objects worked by a craftsman. The Arabic root relates to crafting with tools; σκάφη (from σκάπτω = to dig/hollow) is a hollowed vessel. Shared craft-tool semantic.
